section.title: Peter and John before the Sanhedrin
section.sectionRationale: The healing of the lame man and Peter's sermon in the temple provoke the first official clash between the church and the Sanhedrin, the very council that had condemned Jesus. Peter and John are arrested, examined, and forbidden to speak in the name of Jesus, yet the word keeps spreading and the number of believers grows to five thousand. Threatened and released, the apostles return to the church, which prays not for safety but for boldness, and the passage closes with a picture of a community so gripped by the resurrection that no one counts his possessions his own.
section.thesis: The risen and exalted Christ is Himself the rejected stone now made the cornerstone, the one name under heaven by which anyone is saved, and when His witnesses are threatened for that name the church answers with prayer for boldness, receives a fresh filling of the Spirit, and lives out a resurrection generosity that leaves no one in need.
section.frameNote: Five movements: the arrest at the temple as the resurrection is preached; the examination before the council where Peter names Jesus as the only saving name; the council's helpless threat and the apostles' refusal to be silent; the church's prayer for boldness and its answer; and the shared life of a people transformed by grace.

units.label: Arrest at the temple
units.note: seized for preaching the resurrection, yet the number grows to five thousand

verses.text: While Peter and John were speaking to the people, the priests and the captain of the temple guard and the Sadducees came up to them,
greatly disturbed that they were teaching the people and proclaiming in Jesus the resurrection of the dead.
They seized Peter and John, and because it was evening, they put them in custody until the next day.
But many who heard the message believed, and the number of men grew to about five thousand.

units.label: By what name? The only name that saves
units.note: the rejected stone is the cornerstone; salvation in no one else

verses.text: The next day the rulers, elders, and scribes assembled in Jerusalem,
along with Annas the high priest, Caiaphas, John, Alexander, and many others from the high priest’s family.
They had Peter and John brought in and began to question them: “By what power or what name did you do this?”
Then Peter, filled with the Holy Spirit, said to them, “Rulers and elders of the people!
If we are being examined today about a kind service to a man who was lame, to determine how he was healed,

then let this be known to all of you and to all the people of Israel: It is by the name of Jesus Christ of Nazareth, whom you crucified but whom God raised from the dead, that this man stands before you healed.
This Jesus is ‘the stone you builders rejected, which has become the {{akrogoniaios|cornerstone}}.’
{{soteria|Salvation}} exists in no one else, for there is no other {{onoma|name}} under heaven given to men by which we must be saved.”

units.label: The name forbidden, yet we cannot stop speaking
units.note: the council marvels at their boldness but forbids the name

verses.text: When they saw the {{parresia|boldness}} of Peter and John and realized that they were unschooled, ordinary men, they marveled and took note that these men had been with Jesus.
And seeing the man who had been healed standing there with them, they had nothing to say in response.
So they ordered them to leave the Sanhedrin and then conferred together.
“What shall we do with these men?” they asked. “It is clear to everyone living in Jerusalem that a remarkable miracle has occurred through them, and we cannot deny it.
But to keep this message from spreading any further among the people, we must warn them not to speak to anyone in this name.”
Then they called them in again and commanded them not to speak or teach at all in the name of Jesus.
But Peter and John replied, “Judge for yourselves whether it is right in God’s sight to listen to you rather than God.
For we cannot stop speaking about what we have seen and heard.”
After further threats they let them go. They could not find a way to punish them, because all the people were glorifying God for what had happened.
For the man who was miraculously healed was over forty years old.

units.label: The believers' prayer for boldness
units.note: the church prays not for safety but for courage, and the place is shaken

verses.text: On their release, Peter and John returned to their own people and reported everything that the chief priests and elders had said to them.
When the believers heard this, they lifted up their voices to God with one accord. “Sovereign Lord,” they said, “You made the heaven and the earth and the sea and everything in them.
You spoke by the Holy Spirit through the mouth of Your servant, our father David: ‘Why do the nations rage and the peoples plot in vain?
The kings of the earth take their stand and the rulers gather together against the Lord and against His {{christos|Anointed One}}.’
In fact, this is the very city where Herod and Pontius Pilate conspired with the Gentiles and the people of Israel against Your holy servant Jesus, whom You anointed.
They carried out what Your hand and will had decided beforehand would happen.
And now, Lord, consider their threats, and enable Your servants to speak Your word with complete boldness,
as You stretch out Your hand to heal and perform signs and wonders through the name of Your holy servant Jesus.”
After they had prayed, their meeting place was shaken, and they were all filled with the Holy Spirit and spoke the word of God boldly.

units.label: One in heart and soul
units.note: resurrection generosity leaves no one in need; Barnabas the encourager

verses.text: The multitude of believers was one in heart and soul. No one claimed that any of his possessions was his own, but they shared everything they owned.
With great power the apostles continued to give their testimony about the resurrection of the Lord Jesus. And abundant grace was upon them all.
There were no needy ones among them, because those who owned lands or houses would sell their property, bring the proceeds from the sales,
and lay them at the apostles’ feet for distribution to anyone as he had need.
Joseph, a Levite from Cyprus, whom the apostles called Barnabas (meaning Son of Encouragement),
sold a field he owned, brought the money, and laid it at the apostles’ feet.

onoma.gloss: Name; and in Hebrew and Greek thought far more than a label, it stands for the person himself, his authority and presence. The lame man was healed in the name of Jesus, and Peter presses the point to its edge: there is no other name under heaven by which we must be saved. Salvation is not a technique or a philosophy but a Person, and to invoke His name is to lean the whole weight of one's rescue on Him.

soteria.gloss: Salvation, deliverance, rescue, and the wholeness that follows. The same root that healed the lame man's body reaches to the healing of the whole person before God. Peter declares that this rescue exists in no one else; the God who raised Jesus offers full deliverance in Him alone, and refusing that name is to refuse the only lifeline given to humanity.

akrogoniaios.gloss: The corner, the cornerstone or head of the corner. Quoting Psalm 118:22, Peter tells the builders of Israel that the very stone they rejected in crucifying Jesus is the one God has set as the cornerstone of His new temple, the people He is building. What the authorities discarded God has made the load-bearing stone on which everything now rests.

parresia.gloss: Boldness, frank and fearless speech, the freedom to say openly what must be said. The council marvels that unschooled men speak with such freedom, and later the church prays for still more of it. This boldness is not natural temperament but the fruit of the Spirit and of having been with Jesus; it is the courage to keep naming His name whatever the cost.

christos.gloss: Anointed One, the Christ, the Messiah; the Greek rendering of the Hebrew Mashiach. Praying Psalm 2, the church sees the raging of the nations against the Lord and His Anointed fulfilled in the plot against Jesus, God's holy servant whom He anointed. The rulers meant to destroy the Messiah; instead they carried out exactly what God's hand had planned, and the Anointed reigns.

basic.context: Peter has just healed a man lame from birth and preached Jesus in the temple. Now the temple authorities, especially the Sadducees who denied any resurrection, arrest Peter and John for teaching that Jesus rose from the dead. The next day the same Sanhedrin that condemned Jesus questions them. Peter, filled with the Spirit, tells them plainly that the man was healed by the name of Jesus, the stone they rejected, and that there is no other name that saves. Threatened and warned to stop, the apostles say they cannot be silent, and the church responds by praying for boldness.
basic.hermeneutics: Read this as the first collision between the risen Christ's witnesses and the religious power that killed Him. The heart of it is Peter's speech, and its peak is verse 12: salvation in no other name. Notice how the story keeps contrasting human threat with divine power: the council can imprison and forbid, but it cannot deny the miracle or stop the word. The believers' prayer in verses 24 to 30 shows us how the early church read its own suffering through Scripture, especially Psalm 2.
basic.originalLanguages: Several words carry the passage. The onoma, the name of Jesus, means His very person and authority, and it is the one name that saves, offering soteria, full rescue. Peter quotes Psalm 118 to call Jesus the rejected stone made the akrogoniaios, the cornerstone. The council marvels at the apostles' parresia, their bold and fearless speech, and the church prays Psalm 2 about the plot against God's christos, His Anointed One.
basic.typology: The rejected stone that becomes the cornerstone is a picture Jesus applied to Himself; Israel's builders reject Him, and God makes Him the foundation of a new temple, His people. The persecuted witnesses who cannot stop speaking foreshadow the whole line of faithful confessors. The shared possessions of the church echo the promise that in God's people there would be no poor among them.
basic.christ: Christ is the whole point. He is the name by which the man was healed, the crucified one whom God raised, the rejected stone now the cornerstone, and the only name under heaven that saves. The prayer names Him as God's holy servant and Anointed One, against whom the rulers raged in vain, fulfilling exactly what God had planned. The apostles preach a living Lord, not a dead teacher.
basic.god: God is the Sovereign Lord who made heaven and earth, who spoke by the Spirit through David, and whose hand and will had already decided what the rulers would do. He raises the dead, He heals, and He fills His people with courage. He is not threatened by the Sanhedrin; He answers threats by shaking the room and filling His witnesses afresh with the Spirit.
basic.crossReferences: Psalm 118:22 supplies the rejected stone. Psalm 2:1-2 is the prayer's text about the nations raging against the Anointed. 1 Peter 2:4-8 develops Christ as the living cornerstone. Deuteronomy 15:4 promised there would be no poor among God's people, echoed in the church's generosity.
basic.oneStory: The ascended Christ has poured out His Spirit, and now the gospel meets its first resistance from the very authorities who crucified Him. But the rejected stone is now the cornerstone, and the one name that saves cannot be silenced. From Jerusalem the word keeps spreading, and a new community forms, marked by boldness in witness and grace in sharing, the first outworking of the mission that will reach the ends of the earth.

context.literary: This is the first of several trial scenes in Acts and the first open conflict between the church and the Sanhedrin. It flows directly out of the healing and sermon of chapter 3, and it sets a pattern repeated in chapter 5 and beyond: witness, arrest, testimony before the council, threat, and a church that grows through opposition. The believers' prayer forms a deliberate contrast to the rulers' plotting, and the summary of shared life in verses 32 to 37 rounds off the section and introduces Barnabas, who will matter greatly later.
context.historical: The Sanhedrin was the ruling Jewish council in Jerusalem, led by the high-priestly family. The Sadducees, who dominated the priesthood, denied the resurrection of the dead, which is why the apostles' preaching of the risen Jesus so disturbed them. Annas had been high priest and remained powerful; Caiaphas was his son-in-law and the acting high priest. These are the same authorities who handed Jesus over to Pilate only weeks earlier, so their alarm at His name is acute.
context.canonical: The passage joins the great biblical theme of the rejected stone, running from Psalm 118 through Jesus' own teaching to 1 Peter 2. Peter's claim that there is no other name binds Acts to the exclusivity of Christ found across the New Testament. The prayer's use of Psalm 2 shows the early church reading the Psalms as fulfilled in Jesus, and the shared life fulfills the old promise of a people among whom there is no poor.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: Luke intends to show that the same power that raised Jesus is now at work in His witnesses, that opposition cannot stop the word, and that the exclusive saving name of Jesus must be proclaimed whatever the cost. He also displays the character of the Spirit-filled community: bold in testimony and radically generous in love.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The story describes particular events, an arrest, a trial, a specific outpouring. Yet it carries prescriptive truth: the exclusivity of Christ's name for salvation, the duty to obey God rather than men when they conflict, and the call to bold witness. The pooling of possessions is described as a Spirit-prompted response of love, not laid down as a fixed rule; its abiding principle is that grace produces open-handed care so that no believer is left in need.

debates.question: Does the sharing of possessions in verses 32 to 37 prescribe a form of community that all churches must adopt?

views.label: A description of Spirit-wrought generosity, not a binding structure
views.case: Luke presents the sharing as voluntary and Spirit-prompted; Peter later tells Ananias the land and its proceeds remained his own to give or keep, Acts 5:4, which shows ownership was not abolished. The abiding principle is love that meets need, expressed in ways fitting each setting, not a mandated common purse.

views.label: A pattern the church should seek to embody more fully
views.case: The repeated summaries hold this shared life up as beautiful and normative in spirit; the promise that there would be no poor among God's people is fulfilled here, and later churches are urged to give sacrificially so that there is equality, 2 Corinthians 8. So the passage does press every generation toward real, costly sharing.

debates.bearing: Both agree that the resurrection produces open-handed love that leaves no brother or sister in need; they differ on whether the specific first-Jerusalem form is a required structure or a Spirit-shaped principle to be applied wisely in every context.

study.originalLanguages: The name, onoma, is the beating heart of the passage: not a mere word but the person and authority of Jesus, the only name that brings soteria, salvation. Peter reaches for Psalm 118 to name Jesus the rejected stone now made the akrogoniaios, the cornerstone God has laid. The council is stunned by the apostles' parresia, a bold, free, fearless speech that unschooled men should not possess yet do, because they have been with Jesus. And the church prays Psalm 2, seeing the rulers rage against God's christos, His Anointed, only to accomplish God's own plan.

typology.type: The rejected stone made the cornerstone
typology.fulfillment: Psalm 118:22 is fulfilled as Israel's builders reject Jesus in crucifying Him, and God raises Him and makes Him the cornerstone of a new temple, His people, 1 Peter 2:4-8.

typology.type: The witnesses who cannot stop speaking
typology.fulfillment: The apostles before the council foreshadow the whole company of faithful confessors who must obey God rather than men, a pattern Jesus foretold in Luke 21:12-15.

typology.type: No needy person among the people
typology.fulfillment: Deuteronomy 15:4 promised there would be no poor in God's land; the Spirit-filled church begins to fulfill it as grace overflows in shared possessions.

study.christ: Every line points to Jesus. He is the risen one the Sadducees cannot bear to hear preached, the name that healed the lame man, and the only name under heaven by which anyone is saved. Quoting Psalm 118, Peter names Him the stone the builders rejected, now the cornerstone. The church's prayer calls Him God's holy servant and Anointed One, against whom Herod, Pilate, Gentiles, and Israel conspired, yet only carried out what God's hand had planned beforehand. The apostles' whole testimony is the resurrection of the Lord Jesus, and the power and grace resting on them is His.
study.god: God is the Sovereign Lord, Master of heaven and earth and sea, who spoke by the Spirit through David and whose will governs even the plots of kings. He raised Jesus from the dead, He heals through His name, and He is utterly unthreatened by the council. When His people pray under pressure, He shakes their meeting place, fills them again with His Spirit, and pours out abundant grace, so that their poverty is relieved and their witness made bold. He is a God who acts, not one who merely watches.

crossReferences.note: The stone the builders rejected has become the cornerstone; Peter applies it directly to Jesus.
The nations rage against the Lord and His Anointed; the church prays this text over the plot against Jesus.
Peter later develops Christ as the living cornerstone, precious to believers but a stumbling stone to those who reject Him.
The apostles' resolve here, that they cannot stop speaking, is stated as a principle in the next chapter: we must obey God rather than men.
The promise that there would be no poor among God's people, fulfilled in the church's shared life.

study.oneStory: The exalted Christ has sent His Spirit, and now His witnesses meet the first blow of official opposition. Yet the rejected stone has become the cornerstone, and the one saving name cannot be locked away. Where the rulers plot, God reigns and has already woven their rage into His plan. The church answers threat with prayer, receives fresh boldness, and pours out its goods so that none go without. This is the mission of God moving out from Jerusalem, unstoppable, toward the ends of the earth.

quiz.q: Why did the priests and Sadducees have Peter and John arrested?

options.text: They were disturbed that the apostles taught the people and proclaimed the resurrection in Jesus.
options.note: Verse 2 gives exactly this reason; the Sadducees denied any resurrection.

options.text: The apostles had healed a lame man in the temple.
options.note: The healing sparked the crowd and the sermon, but the stated offense was proclaiming the resurrection in Jesus.

options.text: The apostles were disturbing the peace with a riot.
options.note: There was a crowd, but Luke names the resurrection preaching, not disorder, as the cause of arrest.

options.text: They had spoken against Rome and Caesar.
options.note: The charge was religious, about Jesus and the resurrection, not political rebellion against Rome.

quiz.q: What does Peter declare about salvation before the council?

options.text: There is no other name under heaven given to men by which we must be saved, only Jesus.
options.note: Verse 12 is the peak of Peter's testimony: salvation in no one else.

options.text: Jesus is the rejected stone whom God has made the cornerstone.
options.note: True and closely tied, from verse 11, but the explicit claim about salvation is the exclusive name in verse 12.

options.text: Salvation comes to those who keep the law of Moses faithfully.
options.note: Peter grounds salvation in the name of Jesus, not in law-keeping; this misplaces the emphasis.

options.text: Every sincere path leads to God in the end.
options.note: This directly contradicts Peter, who says there is no other name by which we must be saved.

quiz.q: How did Peter and John respond when ordered never to speak in the name of Jesus?

options.text: They said they could not stop speaking about what they had seen and heard, obeying God rather than men.
options.note: Verses 19 to 20 capture their refusal to be silent.

options.text: They asked the council to judge whether it was right to obey men rather than God.
options.note: They did say this in verse 19, but the fuller answer is their resolve that they cannot stop speaking.

options.text: They agreed to stop for a season to avoid trouble.
options.note: They did not agree to stop; they declared they could not be silent.

options.text: They fled the city to escape the council.
options.note: They did not flee; they returned to the church and prayed for more boldness.

quiz.q: What did the believers pray for after Peter and John were released?

options.text: That God would consider the threats and enable them to speak His word with complete boldness.
options.note: Verses 29 to 30 show they prayed for boldness, not for safety.

options.text: That God would stretch out His hand to heal and do signs through Jesus' name.
options.note: This is part of the prayer, but its central request is for boldness to speak the word.

options.text: That God would remove all persecution and give them a quiet life.
options.note: They did not ask for the threats to be lifted; they asked for courage in the face of them.

options.text: That God would send fire to destroy the Sanhedrin.
options.note: There is no such request; they prayed for boldness and for God's healing power.

quiz.q: What marked the shared life of the believers in verses 32 to 37?

options.text: They were one in heart and soul, sharing everything so that no one among them was in need.
options.note: Verses 32 to 34 describe this unity and generosity, with grace upon them all.

options.text: They pooled their possessions and laid the proceeds at the apostles' feet.
options.note: True, as with Barnabas, but this was the expression of a deeper unity of heart and abundant grace.

options.text: They abolished private ownership as a permanent law for all churches.
options.note: The giving was voluntary and Spirit-prompted; Acts 5:4 shows ownership was not abolished.

options.text: They kept their goods to themselves and cared little for the poor.
options.note: The opposite is true; there were no needy ones among them.

questions: Peter and John were arrested for proclaiming the resurrection. Where in your own life does bearing witness to the risen Jesus feel costly, and what would faithfulness look like there?
Peter says there is no other name under heaven by which we must be saved. How does the exclusivity of Christ sit with you, and how might you hold it with both conviction and compassion?

The council marveled that ordinary, unschooled men spoke with such boldness, and took note that they had been with Jesus. What might change in your witness if being with Jesus were your deepest habit?
When threatened, the church prayed for boldness rather than for safety. What do your own prayers under pressure tend to ask for, and how might this prayer reshape them?
The believers were so gripped by the resurrection that no one counted his possessions his own. Where is God inviting you toward more open-handed generosity so that others' needs are met?
Barnabas earned the name Son of Encouragement. Who has been a Barnabas to you, and whom might God be calling you to encourage in a concrete way this week?

translationNotes.note: The word rendered cornerstone translates a phrase that is literally the head of the corner, quoting Psalm 118:22; it names the chief stone that anchors the structure, which the New Testament applies to Christ.

Unschooled, ordinary men translates terms meaning without formal rabbinic training and private, that is, laymen. It highlights that their boldness came not from education but from having been with Jesus.
The prayer quotes Psalm 2:1-2. Where the text reads Anointed One, the underlying word is Christos, and a footnote notes the alternate renderings His Christ or His Messiah.
The word servant, also used in verse 30, can be rendered child; it echoes the servant of the Lord language of Isaiah applied to Jesus.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Acts 4. Footnote markers, line breaks, and poem speaker markers were dropped when building verse text; poem line text was kept and joined with single spaces. No em or en dashes occur in this chapter. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
